steps.text: Give an initial briefing at the site prior to participation in any emergency response.
Include instruction in the wearing of appropriate personal protective equipment.
Include information on what chemical hazards are involved.
Include instructions on what duties are to be performed.
Ensure all appropriate safety and health precautions are used.
Provide training or demonstrate competency in the area of specialization annually for specialist employees.
Provide training based on the duties and functions to be performed by each responder.
Convey skill and knowledge levels required for all new responders through training before participation in actual emergency operations.
Train employees who participate, or are expected to participate in emergency response in accordance with the required paragraphs.
Provide sufficient training to first responders at the awareness level to demonstrably initiate an emergency response sequence.

constraints.text: Appropriate personal protective equipment must be worn.

steps.text: Review the applicable paragraphs of 29 CFR 1910.120 and the elements of the employer’s occupational safety and health plan.
Review relevant hazards such as, but not limited to, chemical, biological, and radiological exposures; fire and explosion hazards; thermal extremes; and physical hazards.
Review general safety hazards including those associated with electrical hazards, powered equipment hazards, lockout/tagout procedures, motor vehicle hazards, and walking-working surface hazards.
Review confined space hazards and procedures.
Discuss work practices to minimize employee risk from workplace hazards.
Review the emergency response plan and procedures including first aid meeting the requirements of paragraph (p)(8).
Review procedures to minimize exposure to hazardous waste and various types of waste streams, including the materials handling program and spill containment program.
Review hazard communication programs meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.1200.

Review medical surveillance programs meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.120(p)(3) including the recognition of signs and symptoms of overexposure to hazardous substances including known synergistic interactions.
Review decontamination programs and procedures meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.120(p)(4).
Review an employer’s requirements to implement a training program.
Provide classroom or on-the-job training to establish employee proficiency in required work practices.
Certify that each employee has received the required training.
Ensure the certification is maintained for the duration of the employee's employment.
Provide medical services and first aid as required.
Ensure at least two trained persons in first aid, including CPR, are available for field work with two or more employees.
Ensure trained persons are sufficient to reach an employee exposed to electric shock within 4 minutes at fixed work locations.

preconditions.text: If all new employees are trained in first aid within 3 months of hiring, only one trained person needs to be available.
If existing number of employees is insufficient to meet trained person requirement, all employees at the work location shall be trained.

steps.text: Provide employees with information and training at the time of their initial assignment to a work area where benzene is present.
Provide information and training at least annually thereafter if exposures to benzene are above the action level.
Ensure the training program is in accordance with the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.1200(h)(1) and (2).
Provide employees with an explanation of the contents of this section, including Appendices A and B, and indicate where the standard is available.
Describe the medical surveillance program required and explain the information contained in Appendix C.
Train each employee who is employed in a regulated area in accordance with the requirements of this section.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation in the program.
Provide the training program for employees employed in the regulated area as of January 27, 1977 or at the time of initial assignment.
Provide the training program at least annually for all employees employed in the regulated area.
Demonstrate proficiency in standard microbiological practices and techniques.
Have prior experience in the handling of human pathogens or tissue cultures.
Provide a training program to employees who have no prior experience in handling human pathogens.
Maintain training records for 3 years from the date on which the training occurred.
Ensure that all records required to be maintained shall be made available upon request.
Provide employees with information and training on chemical hazards.
Provide information at the time of initial assignment and prior to new exposure situations.
Inform employees of the contents of the standard and its appendices.
Inform employees of the location and availability of the employer's Chemical Hygiene Plan.
Inform employees of permissible exposure limits for OSHA regulated substances or recommended exposure limits for hazardous chemicals.
Inform employees of signs and symptoms associated with exposures to hazardous chemicals.
Inform employees of the location and availability of known reference material on hazardous chemicals.
Conduct employee training that includes methods to detect the presence of hazardous chemicals.
Conduct employee training on physical and health hazards of chemicals in the work area.
Conduct employee training on measures to protect themselves from chemical hazards.
Open the cylinder valve slightly and close immediately.
Open the cylinder valve slowly to prevent damage to the regulator.
Ensure valves on fuel gas cylinders are not opened more than 1-1/2 turns.
Leave a special wrench in position on the stem of the valve while the cylinder is in use.
Ensure at least one special wrench is always available for immediate use with manifolded or coupled cylinders.
Do not place anything on top of a fuel gas cylinder that may damage the safety device or interfere with the valve.
Do not use fuel gas from cylinders through torches or devices equipped with shutoff valves without reducing the pressure through a suitable regulator.
Close the cylinder valve and release the gas from the regulator before removing the regulator.
If there is a leak around the valve stem when the valve is opened, close the valve and tighten the gland nut.
If tightening the gland nut does not stop the leak, discontinue use of the cylinder and tag it for removal.
If fuel gas leaks from the cylinder valve and cannot be shut off, tag the cylinder and remove it from the vessel.

constraints.text: The person cracking the valve shall stand to one side of the outlet, not in front of it.
The cylinder valve shall not be cracked where the gas would reach welding work, sparks, flame or other possible sources of ignition.
Nothing shall be placed on top of a fuel gas cylinder that may damage the safety device or interfere with the quick closing of the valve.

steps.text: Review of the principles and practice for proper selection and use of personal protective equipment.
Review of the principles and practices of establishing exposure zones, proper decontamination and medical surveillance stations and procedures.

Review of the expected hazards including fire and explosions hazards, confined space hazards, electrical hazards, powered equipment hazards, motor vehicle hazards, and walking-working surface hazards.

Awareness and knowledge of the competencies for the Hazardous Materials Technician covered in the National Fire Protection Association’s Standard No. 472, Professional Competence of Responders to Hazardous Materials Incidents.
Review of and demonstration of competency in performing the applicable skills of 29 CFR 1926.65(q).

Hands-on experience with retrieval and use of written and electronic information relative to response decision making including but not limited to the U.S. Department of Transportation’s Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG), manufacturer material safety data sheets, CHEMTREC/CANUTEC, shipper or manufacturer contacts, computer data bases and response models, and other relevant sources of information addressing hazardous substance releases. Familiarization with 29 CFR 1926.60.

Review of the principles and practices for analyzing an incident to determine the hazardous substances present, their physical and chemical properties, and the likely behavior of the hazardous substance and its container, vessel, or vehicle.

Review of the principles and practices for identification of the types of hazardous substance transportation containers, vessels and vehicles involved in the release; selecting and using the various types of equipment available for plugging or patching transportation containers, vessels or vehicles; organizing and directing.
Erect scaffolds under the supervision and direction of a competent person.
Move scaffolds under the supervision and direction of a competent person.
Dismantle scaffolds under the supervision and direction of a competent person.
Alter scaffolds under the supervision and direction of a competent person.
Train each employee who performs work on a scaffold to recognize hazards.

constraints.text: Scaffold activities must be performed only by experienced and trained employees.
Training must be provided by a person qualified in the subject matter.

steps.text: Provide a training program for each employee who might be exposed to fall hazards.
Train each employee to recognize the hazards of falling and in the procedures to minimize these hazards.
Assure that each employee has been trained by a qualified person in the nature of fall hazards.
Assure that each employee is trained in the correct procedures for erecting, maintaining, disassembling, and inspecting fall protection systems.
Train employees in the use and operation of various protection systems.
Ensure employees understand their role in the safety monitoring system when used.
Train employees on the limitations of mechanical equipment during roofing work on low-sloped roofs.
Train employees on the correct procedures for handling and storage of equipment and materials.
Make employees aware of their role in fall protection plans.
Train employees on the standards contained in this subpart.
Prepare a written certification record to verify compliance with training requirements.
Include the name, date(s) of training, and signature in the certification record.
Evaluate and develop controls for occupational health hazards.

A competent person shall evaluate the work area, the projected work practices and the engineering controls, and shall certify in writing that the different or modified controls are adequate to reduce direct and indirect employee exposure to below the PELs under all expected conditions of use.
Before work begins and as needed during the job, a competent person shall conduct an inspection of the worksite and determine that the roofing material is intact.
All employees performing work covered by this paragraph shall be trained in a training program that meets the requirements of paragraph (k)(9)(viii) of this section.
